title: Customer Spend Above the Customer Average
learning_objective: Calculate an average of grouped customer totals by building and validating each table-shaped stage separately.
recommended_lesson: Lesson 4: Subqueries in FROM and Lesson 7: Build and Debug from the Inside Out
why: Business benchmarks are often averages of already-aggregated values, such as the average total spend per customer rather than the average individual order.
prompt: For completed orders only, return customer_id and total_spent for customers whose total spend is above the average completed-order total per purchasing customer. Sort highest total_spent first.
expected_result: 3 rows with `customer_id` and `total_spent`, ordered from the highest total downward.
build_steps: Create one completed-order total per customer and run that summary alone.
Create a second copy of the same summary for the benchmark input.
Calculate the average of those per-customer totals.
Compare each outer customer total with that average benchmark.
Sort the qualifying customer totals descending.
explanation: First create one total per customer. The outer query filters those totals against an average calculated from another copy of the same customer-total summary.
common_mistakes: Calculating average order amount instead of average total spend per customer.
Forgetting the Completed filter in one of the two summaries.
Applying AVG directly to SUM(amount) at the same grouping level.
Losing track of which alias belongs to which stage.
hints: Each summary groups by customer_id and calculates SUM(amount).
The innermost alias should be customer_total so AVG(customer_total) can use it.
takeaways: Average order amount and average customer total are different questions.
When a benchmark depends on grouped totals, aggregate in stages.
reflection_questions: Why must the customer totals be calculated before their average can be calculated?
What does one row of `customer_totals` represent?
stretch_goal: Rewrite the solution with a CTE named `customer_totals`, then compare readability with the nested version.
starter_sql:
-- Write your answer below.
-- Use the task, expected output, and dataset details above.

SELECT
    -- requested columns
FROM orders;
solution_explanation: The business benchmark is not an average order; it is the average of one total per purchasing customer. The solution therefore aggregates in two layers: SUM by customer first, then AVG across those grouped totals.
solution_walkthrough: The outer derived table creates one completed-order total per customer.
The deepest derived table recreates those totals as input to AVG.
The scalar benchmark is compared with each row of the outer customer-total table.
subtitle: Stage 6 • Aggregate in layers
